questions.question_text: How many Principal Brokers can an Arkansas real estate firm have?
questions.explanation: Every Arkansas real estate firm has exactly one Principal Broker who is responsible for all business conducted by the firm and the activities of all licensees.

questions.question_text: A salesperson makes an error in a transaction. Who bears ultimate responsibility?
distractors: The Executive Broker of that office
The client who hired the firm
questions.explanation: The Principal Broker is responsible for all business conducted by the firm and the activities of all licensees. This responsibility cannot be delegated away.

questions.question_text: Can an Arkansas Principal Broker delegate away their ultimate responsibility for firm operations?
questions.correct_answer: No, ultimate responsibility stays with them
distractors: Yes, to any designated Executive Broker
Yes, with prior written AREC approval
Yes, to any licensed supervising attorney
questions.explanation: While a Principal Broker may assign tasks and use Executive Brokers for supervision, they cannot delegate away their ultimate responsibility for the firm's business.

questions.question_text: The Principal Broker must develop and maintain which of the following?
questions.explanation: The Principal Broker must develop and maintain policies and procedures for the firm, including training and supervising all affiliated licensees.

questions.question_text: A Principal Broker's responsibilities include all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: The Principal Broker must supervise licensees, maintain policies, and ensure proper trust account handling, but cannot guarantee transaction outcomes.

questions.question_text: Which broker designation, unique to Arkansas, allows a broker working under a Principal Broker to supervise other licensees?
questions.explanation: The Executive Broker designation is unique to Arkansas: an executive broker works under the principal broker's supervision but may supervise salespersons and other brokers and sign offer-and-acceptance forms as supervising broker. An associate broker holds a broker's license but has no supervisory authority.

questions.question_text: What education must an Executive Broker complete before being designated?
questions.explanation: An Executive Broker must complete the 30-hour broker post-licensure course before receiving the designation, the same course all new brokers must complete. AREC may temporarily waive this for a broker who proves enrollment in an upcoming course.

questions.question_text: A firm has 4 Executive Brokers. Does this violate Arkansas rules?
questions.correct_answer: No, there is no limit on Executive Brokers
distractors: Yes, only 2 are permitted per firm
Yes, only 1 per office is allowed
Yes, AREC must approve more than 3
questions.explanation: There is no limit on how many Executive Brokers a firm can have. Each branch office must have a Designated Executive Broker, but the firm can designate as many as needed.

questions.question_text: A part-time broker in Arkansas must:
questions.correct_answer: Meet the same CE requirements as full-time brokers
distractors: Complete only half the required CE hours
Work a minimum of 20 hours per week
questions.explanation: Part-time brokers are subject to all the same requirements as full-time licensees, including the full 7 hours of annual CE.

questions.question_text: When an Executive Broker is designated, does this absolve the Principal Broker of responsibility?
questions.correct_answer: No, the Principal Broker retains responsibility
distractors: Yes, for that Executive Broker's agents
Yes, for that specific branch office only
Yes, if documented in the firm agreement
questions.explanation: Designating an Executive Broker does NOT absolve the Principal Broker of general responsibility. The PB remains ultimately responsible for the entire firm.

questions.question_text: A salesperson closes a deal without their Principal Broker's knowledge or involvement. This is called:
distractors: Solo brokerage, which requires AREC approval
questions.explanation: Independent dealing — a salesperson acting independently of the principal broker — is prohibited under Arkansas law. All activities must be conducted under broker supervision.

questions.question_text: A Principal Broker discovers a salesperson has been showing properties without broker authorization. The broker should:
questions.correct_answer: Address the violation and enforce supervision policies
distractors: Ignore it if the salesperson made a sale
Report the salesperson to the FBI
questions.explanation: Independent dealing is prohibited. The Principal Broker must enforce supervision policies. The broker cannot terminate a license (only AREC can), but can terminate the employment/affiliation.

questions.question_text: What must each Arkansas branch office where licensees are stationed have?
questions.explanation: Each branch office where licensees are assigned must have a Designated Executive Broker in charge who supervises the agents at that location. The firm still has only one Principal Broker overall.

questions.question_text: The Principal Broker must distinguish between which two types of worker relationships?
questions.explanation: The Principal Broker must properly classify affiliated agents as either employees or independent contractors, as the classification affects tax obligations, supervision, and liability.

questions.question_text: A firm uses unlicensed assistants to help agents with administrative tasks. Who is ultimately responsible for the policies governing these assistants?
distractors: The individual agent who hired them
No oversight is required for assistants
questions.explanation: The Principal Broker is ultimately responsible for firm policies regarding unlicensed staff, even though the hiring agent directs their day-to-day work. Unlicensed assistants must be limited to administrative tasks and cannot perform activities requiring a license.
